title: Use simulated dual-stack binds when using WSL
body:
Resolves a WSL problem where traffic from only one stack is relayed

Fixes #12292

Signed-off-by: Jason T. Greene <jason.greene@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name.  Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body: @Luap99 Ok I switched it to modify the port mapping level instead. The reason I originally did this at the rootlesskit level was when I checked slrirp4netns netns was using rootlessport forwarding as well, so my thinking was 1 and 2 were covered and the issue wasn't problematic for the 3-4 rootful scenarios, so I was just keeping the change narrow. I take your point though that the networking implementations can evolve and expand so really should be handled earlier.

LGTM
but would like a head nod from @Luap99 to make sure his concerns were addressed.

@Luap99 hey, sorry I wasted your time looking at that last change. I didn't catch what should have been an obvious failure on cni/netavark. I rushed moving the code into port mapping and only did one basic rootful test, which was a false positive due to a stale networking config.

Thinking about this in more detail, I don't think it makes sense to apply this workaround across all network/port mapping implementations (explanation to follow). So I have switched my proposal back to the first/previous approach.

The WSL usability issue this change targets is purely around localhost network handling. They proxy directly from Windows localhost, for both IPv4 and IPv6, by discovering bound ports by address and then binding the same address+port pair on the Windows side. The defect in their approach is that they don't replicate dual-stack binds, and Windows defaults to off, so anything doing so will fail ipv4 connections. Unfortunately, since, as you know, the default on Linux (and everything else that chose to follow RF3493) is the opposite, anyone choosing to port map `::` will run into problems. 

Things really fall apart, though, when you combine this with Golang's networking behavior. While many impls would bind "0.0.0.0" (which we also convert to from an empty host IP) as AF_INET, Golang instead converts it to `::` using AF_INET6 with V6ONLY=false. With the behaviors combined, `0.0.0.0`, `::`, and an empty IP from a Golang-based service is unworkable on Windows. Note that since `0.0.0.0` and `::` translate to the same binding on Golang, attempting to bind both will trigger a socket error. To fix that, `0.0.0.0` must have a protocol of "tcp4", which will cause `net.Listen` to use `AF_INET`. 

With the workaround this PR introduced to rootlessport, both IPv4 and IPv6 function correctly,  mirroring the semantics of dual-stack binding. So, on WSL,`127.0.0.1` and `::1` work as expected.

Without any change, IPv4 works with cni/netavark rootfull networking since the port reservation code registers a dummy host binding and maps `0.0.0.0` to "tcp4". IPv6 localhost does not work, and can not work, in this mode since the kernel drops local scope packets before SNAT/MASQ can fire. Also, if you disable port reservation, nothing will work since WSL won't discover the port.

Also, without any change, IPv4 works with port_handler=slirp4netns since it is C-based and uses AF_INET. IPv6 is not supported at all with `add_hostfwd`; setting an IPv6 IP (e.g. `::`) will fail.

Long story short, the solution only makes sense and is needed for the rootless backend. For the others, it is either unnecessary or breaking. A possible complementary change would be to modify the cni/netavark port reservation to bind `0.0.0.0` and `127.0.0.1` whenever `::` or `::1` are set (respectively). This would allow a port mapping of `::` to work with IPv4 on localhost. However, this IMO is less important than getting IPv4 on rootless functioning.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *Luap99*, *n1hility* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> LGTM
> 
> Thanks for your detailed description. I really dislike that we have to fix broken wsl behaviour but here we are.
> 
> I agree now that this is the best way to fix it when iptables/and slirp4netns are not effected.

Thanks @Luap99. I hear you and promise to rip this code out the second WSL has addressed the problem.
